abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) has significant potential in healthcare applications, but its training and deployment faces challenges due to healthcare's diverse data, complex tasks, and the need to preserve privacy. Foundation models that perform well on medical tasks and require less task-specific tuning data are critical to accelerate the development of healthcare AI applications. We introduce MedGemma, a collection of medical vision-language foundation models based on Gemma 3 4B and 27B. MedGemma demonstrates advanced medical understanding and reasoning on images and text, significantly exceeding the performance of similar-sized generative models and approaching the performance of task-specific models, while maintaining the general capabilities of the Gemma 3 base models. For out-of-distribution tasks, MedGemma achieves 2.6-10% improvement on medical multimodal question answering, 15.5-18.1% improvement on chest X-ray finding classification, and 10.8% improvement on agentic evaluations compared to the base models. Fine-tuning MedGemma further improves performance in subdomains, reducing errors in electronic health record information retrieval by 50% and reaching comparable performance to existing specialized state-of-the-art methods for pneumothorax classification and histopathology patch classification. We additionally introduce MedSigLIP, a medically-tuned vision encoder derived from SigLIP. MedSigLIP powers the visual understanding capabilities of MedGemma and as an encoder achieves comparable or better performance than specialized medical image encoders. Taken together, the MedGemma collection provides a strong foundation of medical image and text capabilities, with potential to significantly accelerate medical research and development of downstream applications. The MedGemma collection, including tutorials and model weights, can be found at this https URL.
title: MedGemma Technical Report

abstract:
Recent advances in general medical AI have made significant strides, but
existing models often lack the reasoning capabilities needed for complex
medical decision-making. This paper presents GMAI-VL-R1, a multimodal medical
reasoning model enhanced by reinforcement learning (RL) to improve its
reasoning abilities. Through iterative training, GMAI-VL-R1 optimizes
decision-making, significantly boosting diagnostic accuracy and clinical
support. We also develop a reasoning data synthesis method, generating
step-by-step reasoning data via rejection sampling, which further enhances the
model's generalization. Experimental results show that after RL training,
GMAI-VL-R1 excels in tasks such as medical image diagnosis and visual question
answering. While the model demonstrates basic memorization with supervised
fine-tuning, RL is crucial for true generalization. Our work establishes new
evaluation benchmarks and paves the way for future advancements in medical
reasoning models. Code, data, and model will be released at
\href{this https URL}{this link}.

keyInsights: Reinforcement learning tuning shows clear advantages over supervised fine-tuning for out-of-distribution cases
Chain-of-thought reasoning enhances performance but tends toward shorter reasoning chains
7B parameter models benefit more from reinforcement learning than 3B models
originalProblem: Existing medical AI models rely heavily on pattern memorization rather than true reasoning
Current supervised fine-tuning approaches struggle with generalization and complex medical scenarios
results: Achieved 2-5.56% accuracy improvements across 6 medical benchmarks compared to baseline
Required significantly less training data than current state-of-the-art methods
Enhanced generalization capabilities while maintaining strong performance on in-distribution tasks
solution: Apply reinforcement learning tuning to base Qwen-VL-7B model using Group Relative Policy Optimization
Create GMAI-Reasoning10K dataset with detailed chain-of-thought annotations across 12 imaging modalities
Implement rule-based reward function considering accuracy, format, and redundancy
paper_summary.summary: Shanghai AI Lab and international collaborators develop GMAI-VL-R1, a reinforcement learning-enhanced multimodal medical reasoning model that achieves improved accuracy across multiple medical benchmarks (57.33% on MMMU, 61.01% on OmniMedVQA) by incorporating chain-of-thought reasoning and utilizing a specially curated dataset of 10,000 medical visual question-answer pairs.

title: GMAI-VL-R1: Harnessing Reinforcement Learning for Multimodal Medical  Reasoning

abstract: Biomedical data is inherently multimodal, comprising physical measurements and natural language narratives. A generalist biomedical AI model needs to simultaneously process different modalities of data, including text and images. Therefore, training an effective generalist biomedical model requires high-quality multimodal data, such as parallel image-text pairs. Here, we present PMC-15M, a novel dataset that is two orders of magnitude larger than existing biomedical multimodal datasets such as MIMIC-CXR, and spans a diverse range of biomedical image types. PMC-15M contains 15 million biomedical image-text pairs collected from 4.4 million scientific articles. Based on PMC-15M, we have pretrained BiomedCLIP, a multimodal foundation model, with domain-specific adaptations tailored to biomedical vision-language processing. We conducted extensive experiments and ablation studies on standard biomedical imaging tasks from retrieval to classification to visual question-answering (VQA). BiomedCLIP achieved new state-of-the-art results in a wide range of standard datasets, substantially outperforming prior approaches. Intriguingly, by large-scale pretraining on diverse biomedical image types, BiomedCLIP even outperforms state-of-the-art radiology-specific models such as BioViL in radiology-specific tasks such as RSNA pneumonia detection. In summary, BiomedCLIP is a fully open-access foundation model that achieves state-of-the-art performance on various biomedical tasks, paving the way for transformative multimodal biomedical discovery and applications. We release our models at this https URL to facilitate future research in multimodal biomedical AI.

keyInsights: Pretraining on a vast, diverse, and publicly available biomedical image-text dataset like PMC-15M enables a foundation model to achieve superior generalizability across various biomedical tasks.
Domain-specific architectural adaptations, such as using PubMedBERT for text encoding and increasing text context length, are crucial for effectively processing complex biomedical information.
A generalist biomedical vision-language model can outperform highly specialized models, even in their respective domains, indicating the strong positive transfer from broad, diverse pretraining.
originalProblem: Existing biomedical multimodal datasets were small (thousands to hundreds of thousands of pairs), lacking diversity across image types, and often proprietary, hindering open research.
General-domain vision-language models (e.g., CLIP) performed poorly in the biomedical domain due to significant differences in visual features and linguistic nuances.
Prior biomedical adaptations of vision-language models were either limited by data scarcity, risking catastrophic forgetting, or lacked scalability and generalizability.
results: BiomedCLIP achieved state-of-the-art cross-modal retrieval, with over 77% accuracy for text-to-image top-5 retrieval on a 700k candidate set, outperforming general-domain CLIP and PubMedCLIP.

The model exhibited superior zero-shot and few-shot image classification across five diverse biomedical datasets (e.g., PCam, RSNA Pneumonia), even surpassing radiology-specific models like BioViL on tasks like pneumonia detection.

BiomedCLIP showed improved performance on medical Visual Question Answering (VQA-RAD, SLAKE) datasets, reaching performance comparable to much larger and highly fine-tuned models such as Med-PaLM M, and demonstrated feasibility for privacy-preserving analysis of proprietary data.
solution: Curated PMC-15M, a novel, large-scale, and publicly accessible dataset containing 15 million biomedical image-text pairs extracted from 4.4 million PubMed Central articles.

Developed BiomedCLIP, a contrastive language-image pretraining model specifically adapted for the biomedical domain, using PubMedBERT as the text encoder, an increased text context size (256 tokens), and a Vision Transformer (ViT-B/16) for images.
Conducted extensive evaluation across a unified benchmark of eight standard biomedical vision-language tasks, including cross-modal retrieval, image classification, and medical VQA.
paper_summary.summary: This research introduces BiomedCLIP, a multimodal biomedical foundation model pretrained on PMC-15M, a new dataset comprising 15 million public biomedical image-text pairs. The model demonstrates superior performance across diverse biomedical vision-language tasks, including cross-modal retrieval, image classification, and medical visual question answering, often outperforming existing general-domain and domain-specific models, and shows potential for privacy-preserving analysis of clinical data.

title: BiomedCLIP: a multimodal biomedical foundation model pretrained from fifteen million scientific image-text pairs

abstract:
Recent advances in reasoning-enhanced large language models (LLMs) and
multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) have significantly improved performance in complex
tasks, yet medical AI models often overlook the structured reasoning processes
inherent in clinical practice. In this work, we present ChestX-Reasoner, a
radiology diagnosis MLLM designed to leverage process supervision mined
directly from clinical reports, reflecting the step-by-step reasoning followed
by radiologists. We construct a large dataset by extracting and refining
reasoning chains from routine radiology reports. Our two-stage training
framework combines supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning guided by
process rewards to better align model reasoning with clinical standards. We
introduce RadRBench-CXR, a comprehensive benchmark featuring 59K visual
question answering samples with 301K clinically validated reasoning steps, and
propose RadRScore, a metric evaluating reasoning factuality, completeness, and
effectiveness. ChestX-Reasoner outperforms existing medical and general-domain
MLLMs in both diagnostic accuracy and reasoning ability, achieving 16%, 5.9%,
and 18% improvements in reasoning ability compared to the best medical MLLM,
the best general MLLM, and its base model, respectively, as well as 3.3%, 24%,
and 27% improvements in outcome accuracy. All resources are open-sourced to
facilitate further research in medical reasoning MLLMs.

keyInsights: Process-level supervision, mined from routine clinical reports, is crucial and highly effective for training medical multimodal large language models (MLLMs) with robust, interpretable reasoning abilities.

A combined training strategy of supervised fine-tuning with broad medical domain alignment and reinforcement learning with explicit process rewards is necessary for optimal reasoning and diagnostic performance.
Explicitly modeling the diagnostic reasoning process, akin to human radiologists, leads to enhanced model interpretability, factual correctness, and ultimately, improved diagnostic accuracy.
originalProblem: Medical AI models, especially in diagnostics, often lack explicit, step-by-step reasoning, making them black boxes and hindering interpretability and trust in clinical settings.
Existing medical MLLMs primarily focus on outcome accuracy, overlooking the wealth of structured diagnostic reasoning processes documented in routine clinical reports (findings to impressions).
There was a lack of standardized benchmarks and metrics specifically designed to assess the reasoning abilities of medical MLLMs.
results: ChestX-Reasoner achieved an average RadRScore of 0.531, demonstrating an 18% improvement over its base model and 8.5% over GPT-4o in chest X-ray reasoning ability.
The model showed superior outcome accuracy with a 3.3% overall improvement compared to the existing state-of-the-art medical MLLM, CheXagent-3B, and a 24% improvement over GPT-4o.

Ablation studies confirmed that both supervised fine-tuning with broad domain data and reinforcement learning with process rewards are essential for achieving high-quality reasoning and improved diagnostic accuracy.
solution: Constructed RadRBench-CXR, a reasoning-augmented Visual Question Answering (VQA) benchmark, by mining step-by-step reasoning chains from chest X-ray clinical reports using GPT-4o and human validation.

Developed a two-stage training framework building upon Qwen2VL-7B, employing supervised fine-tuning followed by reinforcement learning with a novel "Process Factuality Reward" to incentivize correct intermediate reasoning steps.
Introduced RadRScore, an automatic evaluation pipeline, to quantitatively measure the quality of the model's intermediate rationales across factuality, completeness, and effectiveness.
paper_summary.summary: ChestX-Reasoner develops a radiology diagnosis multimodal large language model that improves reasoning capabilities by mining step-by-step process supervision directly from clinical reports. This model demonstrates superior performance in both reasoning and diagnostic accuracy compared to existing general and medical MLLMs.

title: ChestX-Reasoner: Advancing Radiology Foundation Models with Reasoning  through Step-by-Step Verification

abstract: Advancements in medical imaging AI, particularly in 3D imaging, have been limited due to the scarcity of comprehensive datasets. We introduce CT-RATE, a public dataset that pairs 3D medical images with corresponding textual reports. CT-RATE comprises 25,692 non-contrast 3D chest CT scans from 21,304 unique patients. Each scan is accompanied by its corresponding radiology report. Leveraging CT-RATE, we develop CT-CLIP, a CT-focused contrastive language-image pretraining framework designed for broad applications without the need for task-specific training. We demonstrate how CT-CLIP can be used in multi-abnormality detection and case retrieval, and outperforms state-of-the-art fully supervised models across all key metrics. By combining CT-CLIP's vision encoder with a pretrained large language model, we create CT-CHAT, a vision-language foundational chat model for 3D chest CT volumes. Finetuned on over 2.7 million question-answer pairs derived from the CT-RATE dataset, CT-CHAT underscores the necessity for specialized methods in 3D medical imaging. Collectively, the open-source release of CT-RATE, CT-CLIP, and CT-CHAT not only addresses critical challenges in 3D medical imaging but also lays the groundwork for future innovations in medical AI and improved patient care.

keyInsights: Large-scale radiology reports serve as a rich and effective source of semantic supervision for training 3D vision-language foundation models, enabling robust zero-shot performance.
Specialized 3D multimodal foundation models (CT-CLIP, CT-CHAT) outperform both supervised 3D baselines and state-of-the-art 2D vision-language models for tasks involving 3D CT data.

Combining 3D vision encoders with large language models through medical instruction tuning can create generalist multimodal AI assistants capable of diverse, clinically relevant tasks like question-answering and detailed report generation.
originalProblem: A significant scarcity of publicly available large-scale 3D medical imaging datasets paired with textual radiology reports, hindering advanced multimodal AI development.
Higher computational demands for 3D models and a limited number of open-source pretrained models for 3D medical imaging compared to 2D counterparts.
Previous 3D medical imaging studies often relied on supervised approaches, limiting detection capabilities to predefined abnormalities and offering only partial data access.
results: CT-CLIP's zero-shot multi-abnormality detection consistently outperformed a state-of-the-art fully supervised 3D model (CT-Net), achieving a mean AUROC 0.102 higher and a mean F1 score 0.050 higher on internal validation, with similar improvements on external datasets.

CT-CHAT surpassed existing 2D vision-language AI assistants and state-of-the-art 3D radiology report generation models across various VQA tasks and clinical efficacy metrics, demonstrating the superiority of 3D-specific multimodal AI.

The open-source CT-RATE dataset and the developed CT-CLIP and CT-CHAT models provide foundational resources that advance the field of 3D medical imaging AI, exhibiting strong generalization and enabling flexible, open-vocabulary inference across diverse clinical data.
solution: Curated and publicly released CT-RATE, a comprehensive dataset of 25,692 non-contrast 3D chest CT scans with paired radiology reports, to address data scarcity.
Developed CT-CLIP, a chest CT-focused contrastive language-image pretraining framework, leveraging radiology reports for self-supervised learning of 3D multimodal embeddings.
Created CT-CHAT, a vision-language foundational chat model for 3D chest CT volumes, extending CT-CLIP's capabilities into an interactive AI assistant for diverse tasks.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the International School of Medicine, Istanbul Medipol University, and the University of Zurich developed CT-RATE, a large-scale, open-source multimodal 3D chest CT dataset, and introduced CT-CLIP and CT-CHAT, generalist foundation models for 3D medical imaging. CT-CLIP achieved superior zero-shot multi-abnormality detection compared to supervised methods, and CT-CHAT outperformed existing 2D vision-language models and 3D report generation methods in various visual question answering tasks.

title: Generalist Foundation Models from a Multimodal Dataset for 3D Computed Tomography

abstract: In this work, we present MedImageInsight, an open-source medical imaging embedding model. MedImageInsight is trained on medical images with associated text and labels across a diverse collection of domains, including X-Ray, CT, MRI, dermoscopy, OCT, fundus photography, ultrasound, histopathology, and mammography. Rigorous evaluations demonstrate MedImageInsight's ability to achieve state-of-the-art (SOTA) or human expert level performance across classification, image-image search, and fine-tuning tasks. Specifically, on public datasets, MedImageInsight achieves SOTA in CT 3D medical image retrieval, as well as SOTA in disease classification and search for chest X-ray, dermatology, and OCT imaging. Furthermore, MedImageInsight achieves human expert performance in bone age estimation (on both public and partner data), as well as AUC above 0.9 in most other domains. When paired with a text decoder, MedImageInsight achieves near SOTA level single image report findings generation with less than 10\% the parameters of other models. Compared to fine-tuning GPT-4o with only MIMIC-CXR data for the same task, MedImageInsight outperforms in clinical metrics, but underperforms on lexical metrics where GPT-4o sets a new SOTA. Importantly for regulatory purposes, MedImageInsight can generate ROC curves, adjust sensitivity and specificity based on clinical need, and provide evidence-based decision support through image-image search (which can also enable retrieval augmented generation). In an independent clinical evaluation of image-image search in chest X-ray, MedImageInsight outperformed every other publicly available foundation model evaluated by large margins (over 6 points AUC), and significantly outperformed other models in terms of AI fairness (across age and gender). We hope releasing MedImageInsight will help enhance collective progress in medical imaging AI research and development.

keyInsights: A generalist approach enables MedImageInsight to operate across 14 diverse medical imaging domains (e.g., X-ray, CT, MRI, histopathology) without requiring domain-specific fine-tuning.
The model generates confidence scores that support ROC curve analysis, a feature essential for regulatory compliance and clinical threshold adjustments.

It incorporates inherent transparency by enabling image-to-image search for explaining classification decisions through k-Nearest Neighbor evidence, and includes novel 3D pre-training for volumetric medical image retrieval.
originalProblem: The rapid growth of medical image data is outpacing the availability of the medical imaging workforce, creating a growing gap in diagnostic capabilities.
Traditional single-task AI models in medical imaging have limited scope and lack generalizability across different modalities or tasks.
Existing foundation models often do not support features critical for clinical adoption, such as regulatory compliance (e.g., ROC curve generation) and decision transparency.
results: MedImageInsight achieves high AUC scores across multiple classification tasks, including 96.7% AUC for distinguishing normal from abnormal chest X-rays.
The model demonstrates superior precision in 3D medical image retrieval for both tumor detection and segmentation compared to other medical foundation models.

It generates competitive medical reports with a lightweight text decoder, and achieves state-of-the-art performance across various tasks using only ~0.7 billion parameters, significantly fewer than comparable solutions (>10B parameters).
solution: MedImageInsight employs a two-tower architecture with a DaViT image encoder and a text encoder, trained using the UniCL (Unified Contrastive Learning) objective function.
The model is designed as a generalist AI capable of performing multiple tasks, including classification, image search (2D and 3D), and report generation, from a single pre-trained model.
It is an open-source model optimized for efficiency, designed to provide high performance across various medical imaging domains with a small computational footprint.
paper_summary.summary: MedImageInsight is an open-source embedding model designed to operate across 14 distinct medical imaging domains without fine-tuning, providing capabilities for classification, image search, and report generation. The model achieves high performance, including a 96.7% AUC on chest X-ray interpretation and superior 3D image retrieval, while being significantly more parameter-efficient than comparable solutions.

title: MedImageInsight: An Open-Source Embedding Model for General Domain Medical Imaging

abstract:
Medical image analysis is essential to clinical diagnosis and treatment,
which is increasingly supported by multi-modal large language models (MLLMs).
However, previous research has primarily focused on 2D medical images, leaving
3D images under-explored, despite their richer spatial information. This paper
aims to advance 3D medical image analysis with MLLMs. To this end, we present a
large-scale 3D multi-modal medical dataset, M3D-Data, comprising 120K
image-text pairs and 662K instruction-response pairs specifically tailored for
various 3D medical tasks, such as image-text retrieval, report generation,
visual question answering, positioning, and segmentation. Additionally, we
propose M3D-LaMed, a versatile multi-modal large language model for 3D medical
image analysis. Furthermore, we introduce a new 3D multi-modal medical
benchmark, M3D-Bench, which facilitates automatic evaluation across eight
tasks. Through comprehensive evaluation, our method proves to be a robust model
for 3D medical image analysis, outperforming existing solutions. All code,
data, and models are publicly available at: https://github.com/BAAI-DCAI/M3D.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
Southern University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: Large-scale, high-quality 3D multi-modal medical datasets can be effectively created through a combination of public data sources, large language model-based instruction generation, and expert filtering.

A 3D Spatial Pooling Perceiver component is critical for efficiently processing high-dimensional 3D image tokens while preserving spatial information, enabling large language models to effectively integrate 3D visual data.

Pre-training the 3D vision encoder with cross-modal contrastive learning and fine-tuning with a promptable segmentation module allows for the development of a generalist 3D medical MLLM capable of diverse tasks.
originalProblem: Existing medical multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) primarily focus on 2D images, neglecting the rich spatial and anatomical information present in ubiquitous 3D medical imaging (CT, MRI).
Current 2D-centric approaches struggle with 3D data, requiring costly slice-by-slice analysis that loses inter-slice context or failing to interpret complex 3D spatial relationships.
There is a lack of large-scale, high-quality 3D multi-modal medical datasets and comprehensive evaluation benchmarks for training and assessing MLLMs tailored to 3D medical images.
results: M3D-LaMed significantly outperforms 2D PMC-CLIP baselines in Image-Text Retrieval, achieving up to 54% higher Recall@10 metrics by leveraging native 3D understanding.

The model demonstrates superior performance in Report Generation (LLM-based evaluation score of 8.49 compared to RadFM's 4.32) and achieves 75.78% mean accuracy on closed-ended VQA, substantially outperforming RadFM's 19.79%.

M3D-LaMed successfully performs both traditional semantic segmentation and referring expression segmentation based on natural language, showing robust generalization abilities on out-of-distribution medical queries.
solution: Introduces M3D-Data, the largest public 3D multi-modal medical dataset with 120,092 3D image-text pairs and 662,000 instruction-response pairs, generated using LLMs and expert review.

Develops M3D-LaMed, a generalist 3D medical MLLM featuring a 3D Vision Transformer encoder, a 3D Spatial Pooling Perceiver for token reduction, LLaMA-2-7B as the LLM, and a promptable 3D segmentation module.

Establishes M3D-Bench, the first comprehensive evaluation framework for 3D medical MLLMs, covering eight tasks across Image-Text Retrieval, Report Generation, Visual Question Answering, Positioning, and Segmentation.
paper_summary.summary: The M3D framework presents a comprehensive approach for 3D medical image analysis using multi-modal large language models, introducing M3D-Data, the largest public 3D multi-modal medical dataset, and M3D-LaMed, a versatile 3D medical MLLM. The framework achieves superior performance across tasks, including an up to 54% improvement in Recall@10 for image-text retrieval and 75.78% accuracy on closed-ended visual question answering.

title:
M3D: Advancing 3D Medical Image Analysis with Multi-Modal Large Language
  Models

abstract:
Recent advances in clinical AI have enabled remarkable progress across many
clinical domains. However, existing benchmarks and models are primarily limited
to a small set of modalities and tasks, which hinders the development of
large-scale multimodal methods that can make holistic assessments of patient
health and well-being. To bridge this gap, we introduce Clinical Large-Scale
Integrative Multimodal Benchmark (CLIMB), a comprehensive clinical benchmark
unifying diverse clinical data across imaging, language, temporal, and graph
modalities. CLIMB comprises 4.51 million patient samples totaling 19.01
terabytes distributed across 2D imaging, 3D video, time series, graphs, and
multimodal data. Through extensive empirical evaluation, we demonstrate that
multitask pretraining significantly improves performance on understudied
domains, achieving up to 29% improvement in ultrasound and 23% in ECG analysis
over single-task learning. Pretraining on CLIMB also effectively improves
models' generalization capability to new tasks, and strong unimodal encoder
performance translates well to multimodal performance when paired with
task-appropriate fusion strategies. Our findings provide a foundation for new
architecture designs and pretraining strategies to advance clinical AI
research. Code is released at this https URL

keyInsights: Multitask pretraining on CLIMB consistently improves performance, particularly in understudied clinical tasks, modalities, and regions (e.g., 32.54% AUC gain in COVID ultrasound).

General-purpose visual architectures like ConvNeXTv2 often outperform specialized clinical encoders in diverse multitask settings, while specialized models like ECG JEPA remain superior for modalities with unique signal characteristics.
The effectiveness of multimodal fusion strategies varies by task complexity, with cross-attention benefiting complex regression and MLP-based concatenation excelling in simpler classification.
originalProblem: Existing clinical AI systems primarily focus on narrow modalities like 2D images or text, preventing holistic patient assessment.
Current multimodal clinical benchmarks lack the diversity and scale to train AI models that integrate heterogeneous data types, such as imaging, physiological signals, and electronic health records.
Research frequently neglects understudied modalities and underrepresented regions, contributing to biases and limiting the global applicability of clinical AI.
results: Multitask pretraining on CLIMB led to substantial performance boosts, including a 32.54% AUC gain for COVID ultrasound and a 23 percentage point improvement in ECG analysis.

General-purpose visual architectures like ConvNeXTv2 surpassed clinical-specific encoders by up to 35.9% in multitask visual tasks, while specialized ECG models achieved 36.8% higher performance than general time series models.
Models pretrained on CLIMB demonstrated strong few-shot transfer capabilities, achieving 28.7% AUC improvement for CT scans and 29.1% for ultrasound imaging in limited data settings.
solution: Developed CLIMB, a 19.01 TB benchmark comprising 4.51 million patient samples from 44 datasets, integrating 1D, 2D, 3D/video, and graph clinical modalities.
The dataset prioritizes diverse tasks, understudied modalities (e.g., EEG, endoscopic videos, graph data), and data from underrepresented global regions to enhance breadth and fairness.
Established comprehensive evaluation protocols to test multitask pretraining, few-shot transfer learning, and multimodal fusion strategies on this extensive dataset.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from MIT, Harvard Medical School, and Stanford University present CLIMB, an extensive 19.01 TB benchmark dataset spanning diverse clinical modalities and regions. This resource enhances multitask pretraining, few-shot transfer learning, and multimodal fusion for clinical AI, demonstrating performance gains in understudied areas and guiding architectural choices.

title: CLIMB: Data Foundations for Large Scale Multimodal Clinical Foundation  Models

abstract: Clinical decision-making routinely demands reasoning over heterogeneous data, yet existing multimodal language models (MLLMs) remain largely vision-centric and fail to generalize across clinical specialties. To bridge this gap, we introduce QoQ-Med-7B/32B, the first open generalist clinical foundation model that jointly reasons across medical images, time-series signals, and text reports. QoQ-Med is trained with Domain-aware Relative Policy Optimization (DRPO), a novel reinforcement-learning objective that hierarchically scales normalized rewards according to domain rarity and modality difficulty, mitigating performance imbalance caused by skewed clinical data distributions. Trained on 2.61 million instruction tuning pairs spanning 9 clinical domains, we show that DRPO training boosts diagnostic performance by 43% in macro-F1 on average across all visual domains as compared to other critic-free training methods like GRPO. Furthermore, with QoQ-Med trained on intensive segmentation data, it is able to highlight salient regions related to the diagnosis, with an IoU 10x higher than open models while reaching the performance of OpenAI o4-mini. To foster reproducibility and downstream research, we release (i) the full model weights, (ii) the modular training pipeline, and (iii) all intermediate reasoning traces at this https URL.

keyInsights: Jointly processing 1D, 2D, and 3D medical data within a unified framework leads to synergistic improvements in diagnostic tasks compared to single-modality or limited multimodal approaches.
Addressing data heterogeneity in clinical datasets through hierarchical reward scaling (DRPO) during RL training significantly boosts performance, particularly in understudied or scarce modalities.

Integrating transparent reasoning traces and visual saliency directly into the model's output is critical for clinician adoption, allowing for verification and understanding of AI-driven diagnostic suggestions.
originalProblem: Existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs) for clinical diagnosis are primarily vision-centric, failing to integrate 1D time-series data with 2D/3D images for holistic assessment.
Medical MLLMs show limited generalization across diverse clinical specialties and demographics due to training on narrow, often single-institution, corpora.
Many AI models in healthcare lack interpretability and transparency, generating
black-box
answers without revealing reasoning, which hinders clinician trust and adoption.
results: DRPO training achieved a 46% higher mean F1 score across medical imaging modalities compared to the best critic-free baseline (GRPO), with significant gains in ultrasound and mammography.
QoQ-Med-7B/32B outperformed all other open-source clinical MLLMs and demonstrated performance comparable to, and often surpassing, closed-source models like GPT-4o-mini.

The model generated high-quality reasoning traces, found relevant by clinical experts, and produced bounding boxes with IoU scores 10 times higher than other open-source models for localizing evidence.
solution: A modular architecture for QoQ-Med was designed to natively ingest and jointly process 1D time-series, 2D images, 3D images, and text inputs, enabling comprehensive reasoning across clinical domains.

Developed Domain-aware Relative Policy Optimization (DRPO), a novel reinforcement learning objective that hierarchically scales rewards based on domain rarity and intra-domain difficulty, mitigating performance imbalance from data heterogeneity.
The model generates an explainable chain-of-thought reasoning trace and highlights salient regions with bounding box annotations to enhance interpretability and build clinician trust.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at MIT Media Lab developed QoQ-Med, the first open-source generalist clinical foundation model capable of jointly processing 1D time-series, 2D images, 3D scans, and textual reports. Its novel Domain-aware Relative Policy Optimization (DRPO) method improved mean F1 scores by 46% over prior critic-free methods, achieving performance comparable to closed-source models.

title: QoQ-Med: Building Multimodal Clinical Foundation Models with Domain-Aware GRPO Training

abstract: We present MedXIAOHE, a medical vision-language foundation model designed to advance general-purpose medical understanding and reasoning in real-world clinical applications. MedXIAOHE achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse medical benchmarks and surpasses leading closed-source multimodal systems on multiple capabilities. To achieve this, we propose an entity-aware continual pretraining framework that organizes heterogeneous medical corpora to broaden knowledge coverage and reduce long-tail gaps (e.g., rare diseases). For medical expert-level reasoning and interaction, MedXIAOHE incorporates diverse medical reasoning patterns via reinforcement learning and tool-augmented agentic training, enabling multi-step diagnostic reasoning with verifiable decision traces. To improve reliability in real-world use, MedXIAOHE integrates user-preference rubrics, evidence-grounded reasoning, and low-hallucination long-form report generation, with improved adherence to medical instructions. We release this report to document our practical design choices, scaling insights, and evaluation framework, hoping to inspire further research.
title: MedXIAOHE: A Comprehensive Recipe for Building Medical MLLMs

abstract:
Many clinical tasks require an understanding of specialized data, such as
medical images and genomics, which is not typically found in general-purpose
large multimodal models. Building upon Gemini's multimodal models, we develop
several models within the new Med-Gemini family that inherit core capabilities
of Gemini and are optimized for medical use via fine-tuning with 2D and 3D
radiology, histopathology, ophthalmology, dermatology and genomic data.
Med-Gemini-2D sets a new standard for AI-based chest X-ray (CXR) report
generation based on expert evaluation, exceeding previous best results across
two separate datasets by an absolute margin of 1% and 12%, where 57% and 96% of
AI reports on normal cases, and 43% and 65% on abnormal cases, are evaluated as
"equivalent or better" than the original radiologists' reports. We demonstrate
the first ever large multimodal model-based report generation for 3D computed
tomography (CT) volumes using Med-Gemini-3D, with 53% of AI reports considered
clinically acceptable, although additional research is needed to meet expert
radiologist reporting quality. Beyond report generation, Med-Gemini-2D
surpasses the previous best performance in CXR visual question answering (VQA)
and performs well in CXR classification and radiology VQA, exceeding SoTA or
baselines on 17 of 20 tasks. In histopathology, ophthalmology, and dermatology
image classification, Med-Gemini-2D surpasses baselines across 18 out of 20
tasks and approaches task-specific model performance. Beyond imaging,
Med-Gemini-Polygenic outperforms the standard linear polygenic risk score-based
approach for disease risk prediction and generalizes to genetically correlated
diseases for which it has never been trained. Although further development and
evaluation are necessary in the safety-critical medical domain, our results
highlight the potential of Med-Gemini across a wide range of medical tasks.

keyInsights: Fine-tuning a generalist large multimodal model (LMM) like Gemini with diverse medical data can effectively specialize it for complex clinical tasks, demonstrating progress towards "Generalist Medical AI."
Expert human evaluation is crucial for accurately assessing clinical utility in generative tasks like medical report writing, revealing insights into performance beyond automated metrics.

Leveraging existing free-text medical data (e.g., radiology reports) for fine-tuning is a scalable and data-efficient approach for developing powerful medical AI models, reducing the need for expensive, de novo expert labeling.
originalProblem: Traditional AI in medicine typically focused on narrow tasks with single input and output types, failing to capture the inherent multimodal complexity of medical data.

The full potential of general-purpose large multimodal models (LMMs) like Gemini in the specialized and safety-critical medical domain remained largely underexplored due to challenges in domain-specific optimization and a lack of diverse, clinically-grounded evaluations.
Existing public medical AI benchmarks often contained erroneous labels or limited scope, hindering robust comparison and accurate assessment of model capabilities.
results: Med-Gemini-2D reports were rated "equivalent or better" than original radiologist reports by experts in up to 96% of normal CXR cases and 65% of abnormal CXR cases on out-of-distribution data, with 88% overall clinically acceptable.
Med-Gemini-3D demonstrated the first LMM-based report generation for 3D CT volumes, with 53% of its generated reports deemed clinically acceptable by experts.

Med-Gemini-Polygenic outperformed standard linear polygenic risk score methods for disease risk prediction across most in-distribution health outcomes and showed generalization to genetically correlated diseases.
solution: Fine-tuned Google's Gemini 1.5 Pro into specialized Med-Gemini variants (Med-Gemini-2D, Med-Gemini-3D, Med-Gemini-Polygenic) using a massive and diverse dataset of over 7 million medical samples.
Leveraged a data-efficient approach by predominantly using free text paired with medical data (e.g., existing radiology reports), eliminating the need for extensive expert labeling of training data.

Adapted Gemini's architecture to handle 3D medical data by ingeniously treating the depth dimension as a time axis for sequential processing and employed specialized vision encoders for different medical modalities.
paper_summary.summary: Google Research and DeepMind developed Med-Gemini, a family of large multimodal models, by fine-tuning Gemini 1.5 Pro on a vast collection of medical data, achieving a new standard for expert-evaluated chest X-ray report generation (up to 96% clinically acceptable) and introducing the first LMM-based report generation for 3D computed tomography volumes.

title: Advancing Multimodal Medical Capabilities of Gemini

abstract: Recent advances in generative medical models are constrained by modality-specific scenarios that hinder the integration of complementary evidence from imaging, pathology, and clinical notes. This fragmentation limits their evolution into foundation models that can learn and reason across the full spectrum of biomedical data. We propose MeDiM, the first medical discrete diffusion model that learns shared distributions across modalities without modality-specific components. MeDiM unifies multiple generative tasks: translating between images and text, and jointly producing image-report pairs across domains in response to prompts. Built on a discrete diffusion framework, MeDiM bridges vision and language representations through a shared probabilistic space. To enable unified and flexible medical generation, we employ a multimodal large language model (MLLM) as the diffusion backbone, leveraging its prior knowledge and cross-modal reasoning. Two key designs are introduced: (1) removing the causal attention mask for bidirectional context, and (2) injecting continuous timestep embeddings for diffusion awareness. Experiments demonstrate high-fidelity medical generation (FID 16.60 on MIMIC-CXR and FID 24.19 on PathGen) and accurate report generation (METEOR 0.2650 and 0.2580). Jointly generated image-report pairs further enhance downstream performance (plus6.43 percent BLEU-1, plus18.57 percent BLEU-2, plus31.58 percent BLEU-3, plus4.80 percent METEOR), showing that MeDiM supports coherent and clinically grounded multimodal outputs.

keyInsights: MLLMs, with their strong cross-modal alignment priors and unified token representation, are superior backbones for discrete diffusion models in medical multimodal generation.
Adapting autoregressive MLLMs for diffusion denoising requires architectural modifications like causal mask removal and explicit temporal conditioning via timestep embeddings and AdaLN.
Unified modeling of shared distributions for medical images and reports can be achieved without modality-specific components, leading to high-fidelity and clinically consistent multimodal content.
originalProblem: Medical AI models are largely fragmented, operating on isolated modalities and limiting the integration of diverse biomedical evidence.
The absence of unified models hinders the development of true 'foundation models' in medicine that can learn and predict across the full spectrum of biomedical knowledge.
Existing methods struggle to achieve strict semantic and visual consistency between modalities, which is critical for generating clinically grounded medical outputs.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art FID scores for medical image generation: 16.60 on MIMIC-CXR and 24.19 on PathGen, outperforming existing baselines.
Demonstrated competitive performance in medical report generation, yielding reports with reduced semantic redundancy and accurate clinical details (e.g., 0.265 METEOR on MIMIC-CXR).

Generated high-quality, cross-modally consistent image-report pairs that, when used for data augmentation, improved downstream VLM performance by up to +31.58% BLEU-3 in pathology visual analysis tasks.
solution: Developed MeDiM, the first medical discrete diffusion model that uses a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) as its backbone for unified image and text synthesis.

Adapted discrete diffusion models to medical data by tokenizing images (VQ-VAE) and reports (standard tokenizer) into a single unified sequence, then corrupting it with a mask-based forward diffusion process.

Integrated the MLLM backbone with specific architectural designs: removing the causal attention mask for bidirectional attention, adding continuous timestep embeddings, and incorporating Adaptive Layer Normalization (AdaLN) for robust cross-modal consistency.
paper_summary.summary: MeDiM introduces the first medical discrete diffusion model, leveraging a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) backbone for unified medical image and report generation. The system achieves state-of-the-art performance in medical image synthesis, competitive report generation, and produces high-quality multimodal pairs that boost downstream vision-language model performance by up to +31.58% on pathology visual analysis tasks.

title: Discrete Diffusion Models with MLLMs for Unified Medical Multimodal Generation

abstract: Medical imaging provides critical evidence for clinical diagnosis, treatment planning, and surgical decisions, yet most existing imaging models are narrowly focused and require multiple specialized networks, limiting their generalization. Although large-scale language and multimodal models exhibit strong reasoning and multi-task capabilities, real-world clinical applications demand precise visual grounding, multimodal integration, and chain-of-thought reasoning. We introduce Citrus-V, a multimodal medical foundation model that combines image analysis with textual reasoning. The model integrates detection, segmentation, and multimodal chain-of-thought reasoning, enabling pixel-level lesion localization, structured report generation, and physician-like diagnostic inference in a single framework. We propose a novel multimodal training approach and release a curated open-source data suite covering reasoning, detection, segmentation, and document understanding tasks. Evaluations demonstrate that Citrus-V outperforms existing open-source medical models and expert-level imaging systems across multiple benchmarks, delivering a unified pipeline from visual grounding to clinical reasoning and supporting precise lesion quantification, automated reporting, and reliable second opinions.

keyInsights: Tightly integrating pixel-level image grounding with multimodal chain-of-thought reasoning is crucial for achieving physician-like diagnostic inference in medical AI.
A dual image encoder design effectively balances the conflicting requirements of high-level semantic understanding for reasoning tasks and low-level pixel fidelity for segmentation.

A carefully structured multi-stage training curriculum, from concept alignment to specialized fine-tuning, is vital for building robust multimodal medical foundation models without performance degradation across tasks.
originalProblem: Traditional medical AI models are often task-specific and lack generalization across diverse clinical scenarios, leading to fragmented solutions.
Existing multimodal large language models struggle with precise visual grounding, failing to effectively connect high-level reasoning with pixel-level features in medical images.
The scarcity of high-quality, multimodal medical datasets with explicit reasoning paths and pixel-level annotations hinders the development of comprehensive medical AI systems.
results: Citrus-V 33B achieved new state-of-the-art results across 16 benchmarks, including medical textual QA (e.g., top on 4/7 datasets), visual QA (SOTA on VQA-RAD, SLAKE, PMC-VQA), and document understanding (outperforming Qwen2.5-VL by ~20-21%).

It demonstrated substantial improvements in medical image report generation on CheXpert Plus (e.g., +31.24% CIDEr for 33B) with multimodal chain-of-thought reasoning, effectively grounding lesions during report generation.

The model achieved expert-level performance in medical image detection and segmentation, outperforming specialized expert models on 7/8 2D image segmentation datasets and MedPlib 14B on MeCOVQA-G+ seg for CT.
solution: Citrus-V developed a unified architecture integrating a Qwen2.5-VL-based Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) with a SAM2-inspired segmentation model, utilizing dual image encoders for distinct high-level reasoning and low-level pixel fidelity.

A four-stage progressive training paradigm was implemented, starting with concept alignment and advancing through comprehension enhancement, instruction fine-tuning, and dedicated segmentation refinement to mitigate negative transfer.
Extensive data curation and novel synthesis pipelines generated medical visual reasoning data, document understanding QA, and high-fidelity pseudo-labels for image detection and segmentation tasks.
paper_summary.summary: Citrus-V is a medical foundation model designed to unify pixel-level image grounding with multimodal clinical reasoning for diagnostic tasks. It achieved state-of-the-art performance across diverse medical textual QA, visual QA, document understanding, report generation, detection, and segmentation benchmarks.

title: Citrus-V: Advancing Medical Foundation Models with Unified Medical Image Grounding for Clinical Reasoning

abstract: Multimodal large language models have advanced rapidly, but their adoption in medicine is constrained by limited domain coverage, imperfect modality alignment, and insufficient grounded reasoning. We introduce MedMO, a medical multimodal foundation model built on a general MLLM architecture and trained exclusively on large-scale domain-specific data. MedMO uses a multi-stage training recipe that includes cross-modal pretraining to align heterogeneous visual encoders with a medical language backbone, instruction tuning with multi-task supervision spanning captioning, VQA, report generation, retrieval, and bounding-box disease localization, and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards that combine factuality checks with a box-level GIoU signal to improve spatial grounding and step-by-step reasoning in challenging clinical settings. Across modalities and tasks, MedMO surpasses strong open-source medical baselines. MedMO-8B-Next achieves consistent gains on VQA benchmarks, improving by 6.6% on average over Fleming-VL-8B, including gains of 6.0% on MMMU-Med, 9.8% on PMC-VQA, and 21.3% on MedXpertQA. On text-based QA, it improves by 14.4% over Fleming-VL-8B, driven by gains of 8.4% on MMLU-Med and 30.1% on MedQA. For medical report generation, it improves by 6.7% on MIMIC-CXR. MedMO-8B-Next also demonstrates strong grounding performance, reaching 56.1 IoU on Bacteria, which is a 47.8 IoU gain over Fleming-VL-8B. At smaller scale, MedMO-4B-Next remains competitive and exceeds Fleming-VL-8B across VQA, QA, and report generation. Evaluations spanning radiology, ophthalmology, and pathology microscopy further confirm broad cross-modality generalization. Project is available at this https URL

organization_info.name: Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence

keyInsights: A multi-stage post-training pipeline, encompassing diverse medical SFT, high-resolution visual understanding, and instruction tuning, effectively builds a robust generalist medical MLLM.
Integrating a verifiable bounding-box reward during the reinforcement learning stage explicitly enhances spatial grounding and localization performance in medical images.
Curating a large-scale, diverse, and high-quality multimodal medical dataset is crucial for achieving comprehensive domain understanding and strong cross-modal generalization capabilities.
originalProblem: General-purpose Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) struggle with precise, domain-specific interpretation and contextualization of medical images, often yielding uncertain or hallucinatory outputs.
Existing medical MLLMs frequently lack accurate domain grounding due to reliance on data distilled from proprietary systems, leading to less reliable outputs and increased hallucination risk.

Many current models specialize in individual tasks or narrow subsets of medical modalities, limiting their ability to achieve unified, cross-modal generalization across diverse real-world healthcare imaging types.
results: MedMO-8B-Next achieved the highest average VQA score of 72.7%, outperforming Fleming-VL-8B by 6.6%, and led in Text QA with an average of 60.1%, surpassing Fleming-VL-8B by 14.4%.
For report generation, MedMO-8B-Next attained a CIDEr score of 272.1 on Med-Trinity, substantially higher than the next best open-source model (Qwen2.5VL-7B at 81.5).
In grounding tasks, MedMO-8B-Next achieved the best overall average IoU score of 56.8%, significantly higher than Fleming-VL-8B (17.2%) and Lingshu-7B (13.9%).
solution: MedMO employs a four-stage sequential post-training pipeline, initialized from Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct, leveraging large-scale, domain-specific data to progressively enhance capabilities.

The pipeline includes general medical supervised fine-tuning (SFT), quality medical image SFT incorporating bounding-box grounding, instruction tuning for human-style reasoning, and reinforcement learning (RL) with a comprehensive reward function.
An extensive, harmonized dataset comprising over 26 million multimodal medical samples from 45 datasets was curated to provide broad domain coverage across various imaging modalities.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence developed MedMO, an open-source multimodal large vision-language model (VLM) trained on over 26 million medical samples. The model demonstrates superior performance across medical VQA, report generation, and grounding tasks, achieving up to 6.6% higher average VQA scores and significantly improved IoU scores for grounding compared to existing baselines.

title: MedMO: Grounding and Understanding Multimodal Large Language Model for Medical Images

abstract:
Despite significant advancements in general AI, its effectiveness in the
medical domain is limited by the lack of specialized medical knowledge. To
address this, we formulate GMAI-VL-5.5M, a multimodal medical dataset created
by converting hundreds of specialized medical datasets with various annotations
into high-quality image-text pairs. This dataset offers comprehensive task
coverage, diverse modalities, and rich image-text data. Building upon this
dataset, we develop GMAI-VL, a general medical vision-language model, with a
three-stage training strategy that enhances the integration of visual and
textual information. This approach significantly improves the model's ability
to process multimodal data, supporting accurate diagnoses and clinical
decision-making. Experiments show that GMAI-VL achieves state-of-the-art
performance across various multimodal medical tasks, including visual question
answering and medical image diagnosis.

keyInsights: A carefully curated, large-scale multimodal medical dataset, generated with annotation-guided AI, is fundamental for effectively integrating diverse medical information and bridging the domain gap in medical AI.

A progressive, multi-stage training strategy is essential for fine-tuning general LVLMs for medical applications, enabling effective alignment of visual and linguistic features and enhancing complex instruction following.

Integrating a wide array of medical modalities and specialized tasks within a single model and dataset framework significantly improves the model's generalization capabilities across various clinical scenarios.
originalProblem: General Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) lack specialized medical knowledge, which limits their applicability to complex diagnostic and treatment decision-making.

Existing medical vision-language datasets often have restricted modality coverage (e.g., single imaging type), limited data sources, or insufficient task diversity, hindering the development of truly general medical AI.
Prior adaptation strategies for medical LVLMs struggle to effectively bridge the domain gap between natural and medical images or to align diverse medical modalities with varied textual data.
results: GMAI-VL achieved state-of-the-art results on several medical VQA benchmarks, including VQA-RAD (66.3%), PMC-VQA (54.3%), SLAKE (72.9%), and set a new benchmark on OmniMedVQA with an average accuracy of 88.48%.

The model demonstrated strong performance on the GMAI-MMBench (62.43% overall), ranking first or second in 16 of 20 categories, and achieved top scores in three categories of the MMMU Health & Medicine track (51.3% average).

Ablation studies confirmed that both the GMAI-VL-5.5M dataset and the proposed three-stage training strategy are crucial, independently contributing to the enhanced performance and robustness of the GMAI-VL model across diverse medical tasks.
solution: A comprehensive multimodal medical dataset, GMAI-VL-5.5M, was created by converting 219 specialized medical datasets across 13 modalities and 18 specialties into 5.5 million high-quality, annotation-guided image-text pairs using GPT-4o.
A general medical vision-language model, GMAI-VL, was developed based on the LLaVA architecture, incorporating InternLM2.5-7B as the LLM and a CLIP-based vision encoder.

A three-stage training strategy was designed, progressing from shallow alignment (MLP only), to deep alignment (MLP + vision encoder), and finally to instruction tuning (entire model), using a total of 11.7 million medical image-text samples.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Shanghai AI Lab and other institutions developed GMAI-VL, a large vision-language model, alongside GMAI-VL-5.5M, a comprehensive multimodal medical dataset comprising 5.5 million high-quality samples. The GMAI-VL model, employing a three-stage training strategy, achieved leading performance across various medical visual question answering and multimodal benchmarks, demonstrating enhanced understanding of diverse medical imaging and textual data.

title: GMAI-VL & GMAI-VL-5.5M: A Large Vision-Language Model and A  Comprehensive Multimodal Dataset Towards General Medical AI

abstract: The rapid growth of medical imaging has fueled the development of Foundation Models (FMs) to reduce the growing, unsustainable workload on radiologists. While recent FMs have shown the power of large-scale pre-training to CT and MRI analysis, there remains significant room to optimize how these models learn from complex radiological volumes. Building upon the Curia framework, this work introduces Curia-2, which significantly improves the original pre-training strategy and representation quality to better capture the specificities of radiological data. The proposed methodology enables scaling the architecture up to billion-parameter Vision Transformers, marking a first for multi-modal CT and MRI FMs. Furthermore, we formalize the evaluation of these models by extending and restructuring CuriaBench into two distinct tracks: a 2D track tailored for slice-based vision models and a 3D track for volumetric benchmarking. Our results demonstrate that Curia-2 outperforms all FMs on vision-focused tasks and fairs competitively to vision-language models on clinically complex tasks such as finding detection. Weights will be made publicly available to foster further research.

keyInsights: Adapting self-supervised learning strategies with domain-specific augmentations (like content-aware cropping) and regularization (SigReg loss) is crucial for effective representation learning and training stability in radiology.
The refined methodology enabled successful scaling of multi-modal CT and MRI foundation models up to 1.3 billion parameters, a first for this domain, although gains diminished at the largest scale.
Pre-training at lower resolutions (256x256) combined with high-resolution fine-tuning is an efficient strategy for developing scalable radiological foundation models.
originalProblem: Existing self-supervised learning strategies (e.g., DINOv2), designed for natural images, are inefficient and suboptimal when directly applied to complex radiological data due to issues like uninformative background and unique volumetric structures.
Scaling multi-modal CT and MRI foundation models to billion-parameter architectures had not been effectively demonstrated in the radiology domain.
A standardized and comprehensive evaluation framework was needed to rigorously assess the clinical utility and generalizability of emerging radiological foundation models.
results: Curia-2 L and Curia-2 g achieved an average score of 88.5% on the 2D evaluation track, establishing new state-of-the-art performance among competing 2D foundation models.
Curia-2 L attained the highest overall average score on the 3D evaluation track at 88.6%, improving its predecessor (Curia L) by 4.1 percentage points.
Curia-2 models, particularly Curia-2 g, demonstrated faster convergence and required fewer samples in few-shot learning scenarios, indicating superior data efficiency.
solution: Implemented content-aware cropping and anatomically-guided masking to focus pre-training on clinically relevant regions and stabilize training, especially at lower resolutions.
Replaced the KoLeo loss with SigReg loss to regularize the global latent geometry, allowing clinically similar structures to remain clustered while maintaining a dense embedding space.
Scaled Vision Transformer architectures up to 1.3 billion parameters (Curia-2 g) and adopted a multi-stage training approach including high-resolution fine-tuning.
paper_summary.summary: Raidium researchers developed Curia-2, a family of multi-modal radiology foundation models, by refining self-supervised learning for CT and MRI data and scaling architectures up to 1.3 billion parameters. The models achieve new state-of-the-art performance on 2D and 3D radiological tasks within an extended CuriaBench, demonstrating improved representation quality and few-shot learning capabilities.

title: Curia-2: Scaling Self-Supervised Learning for Radiology Foundation Models

abstract: Medical image analysis is essential in modern healthcare. Deep learning has redirected research focus toward complex medical multimodal tasks, including report generation and visual question answering. Traditional task-specific models often fall short in handling these challenges. Large vision-language models (LVLMs) offer new solutions for solving such tasks. In this study, we build on the popular LLaVA framework to systematically explore model architectures and training strategies for both 2D and 3D medical LVLMs. We present extensive empirical findings and practical guidance. To support reproducibility and future research, we release a modular codebase, MedM-VL, and two pre-trained models: MedM-VL-2D for 2D medical image analysis and MedM-VL-CT-Chest for 3D CT-based applications. The code is available at: this https URL
title: MedM-VL: What Makes a Good Medical LVLM?

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs), known for their versatility in textual data, are increasingly being explored for their potential to enhance medical image segmentation, a crucial task for accurate diagnostic imaging. This study explores enhancing Vision Transformers (ViTs) for medical image segmentation by integrating pre-trained LLM transformer blocks. Our approach, which incorporates a frozen LLM transformer block into the encoder of a ViT-based model, leads to substantial improvements in segmentation performance across various medical imaging modalities. We propose a Hybrid Attention Mechanism that combines global and local feature learning with a Multi-Scale Fusion Block for aggregating features across different scales. The enhanced model shows significant performance gains, including an average Dice score increase from 0.74 to 0.79 and improvements in accuracy, precision, and the Jaccard Index. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of LLM-based transformers in refining medical image segmentation, highlighting their potential to significantly boost model accuracy and robustness. The source code and our implementation are available at: this https URL

keyInsights: Pre-trained general-purpose LLMs can act as "residual attention boosters" for ViTs, effectively refining visual features and improving segmentation without explicit textual input.
The benefits from LLM integration stem from their abstract relational structures and extensive pre-training, as general LLMs perform comparably to domain-specific medical LLMs for this visual task.
Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (LoRA) is crucial for efficiently adapting and integrating large, frozen LLM components into vision models, preserving knowledge with minimal computational overhead.
originalProblem: Medical image segmentation struggles with noisy, low-resolution, and highly variable images, making accurate and consistent results challenging.
Advanced deep learning models like ViTs require extensive, meticulously labeled datasets, which are scarce and costly to obtain in clinical settings.
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) lack the capacity to model long-range contextual dependencies crucial for complex anatomical understanding.
results: MedVisionLlama consistently surpassed the ViT-Baseline and several state-of-the-art models across all ten Medical Segmentation Decathlon tasks, showing statistically significant improvements in 41 out of 60 comparisons.

The model demonstrated superior performance and faster convergence in data-constrained few-shot learning scenarios (10% and 30% of data), highlighting its data efficiency and generalization capabilities.

Ablation studies confirmed that performance gains were attributable to the specific pre-trained knowledge within the Llama weights and their role in feature refinement, rather than merely increased model complexity.
solution: MedVisionLlama introduces a hybrid architecture that integrates a single frozen transformer block from a pre-trained Llama LLM into a ViT-based encoder-decoder segmentation model.
Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA)-based dimension mapping layers efficiently adapt the ViT's visual features to the LLM block and project the refined embeddings back, minimizing trainable parameters.
The approach leverages the LLM's pre-trained contextual reasoning to implicitly enhance visual feature representations without requiring explicit language prompts or multimodal pre-training.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from McGill University developed MedVisionLlama, an architecture that integrates frozen Large Language Model (LLM) layers into Vision Transformers (ViTs) for 3D medical image segmentation. This approach improves segmentation accuracy and data efficiency by leveraging LLM's implicit knowledge, outperforming baselines across diverse tasks.

title: MedVisionLlama: Leveraging Pre-Trained Large Language Model Layers to Enhance Medical Image Segmentation

abstract:
Current medical AI systems are often limited to narrow applications,
hindering widespread adoption. We present MedVersa, a generalist foundation
model trained on tens of millions of compiled medical instances. MedVersa
unlocks generalist learning from multimodal inputs and outputs, representing
the first example of a generalist model reaching competitive performance with
leading specialized solutions across a variety of medical imaging scenarios.
MedVersa achieves state-of-the-art performance in nine tasks, sometimes
outperforming counterparts by over 10%. Radiologist evaluation shows
MedVersa-generated reports get superior performance in 95% of normal studies,
while matching or exceeding human reports in 71% of cases overall. User studies
showed notable reductions in report writing time and discrepancies with the use
of MedVersa. Our findings underscore the value of flexible, multimodal AI
systems in advancing medical image interpretation and supporting clinical
expertise.

organization_info.name: Jawaharlal Institute of Postgraduate Medical Education and Research

keyInsights: Training a generalist model across both vision-language and vision-centric tasks provides synergistic benefits, improving overall performance by 5.3% to 6.4%.
A large language model can act as a dynamic orchestrator, using latent embeddings to prompt specialized vision modules for precise vision-centric tasks.
The ability to learn from multimodal inputs and produce diverse outputs (text, masks, boxes) within one model offers a more holistic approach to medical image interpretation.
originalProblem: Medical AI has largely produced specialized systems for narrow tasks, limiting their real-world clinical applicability and widespread adoption.
Current generalist medical AI models struggle with vision-centric tasks like object detection and segmentation, which are vital for detailed image analysis.
The fragmentation of AI tools in healthcare creates inefficiencies and hinders seamless integration into diverse clinical workflows.
results: MedVersa achieved state-of-the-art performance in nine medical image interpretation tasks, including report generation and segmentation, with 10x fewer parameters than some comparable models.
Board-certified radiologists found MedVersa-generated reports matched or surpassed human-written reports in 71% of cases, with 95% equivalence/preference for normal studies.
A user study indicated MedVersa-assisted reporting reduced radiologist reporting time by up to 50 minutes per 100 studies and lowered urgent/emergent discrepancies.
solution: MedVersa, a generalist foundation model, integrates an LLM-based orchestrator with specialized vision modules to handle both multimodal inputs and outputs.
The model was trained on MedInterp, a curated dataset of 29 million instances spanning 11 tasks and 7 imaging modalities, balanced for vision-language and vision-centric data.
It employs a 'domain-aware minibatch gradient descent' strategy and 'referring image instruction tuning' for robust and efficient multimodal, multitask learning.
paper_summary.summary: MedVersa is a generalist foundation model for medical image interpretation that integrates multimodal inputs and outputs across various tasks. The model achieves state-of-the-art performance in nine diverse tasks, including report generation and image segmentation, while reducing radiologist reporting time by up to 50 minutes per 100 studies and lowering urgent/emergent discrepancies.

title: MedVersa: A Generalist Foundation Model for Medical Image Interpretation

abstract: Medicine is inherently multimodal, with rich data modalities spanning text, imaging, genomics, and more. Generalist biomedical artificial intelligence (AI) systems that flexibly encode, integrate, and interpret this data at scale can potentially enable impactful applications ranging from scientific discovery to care delivery. To enable the development of these models, we first curate MultiMedBench, a new multimodal biomedical benchmark. MultiMedBench encompasses 14 diverse tasks such as medical question answering, mammography and dermatology image interpretation, radiology report generation and summarization, and genomic variant calling. We then introduce Med-PaLM Multimodal (Med-PaLM M), our proof of concept for a generalist biomedical AI system. Med-PaLM M is a large multimodal generative model that flexibly encodes and interprets biomedical data including clinical language, imaging, and genomics with the same set of model weights. Med-PaLM M reaches performance competitive with or exceeding the state of the art on all MultiMedBench tasks, often surpassing specialist models by a wide margin. We also report examples of zero-shot generalization to novel medical concepts and tasks, positive transfer learning across tasks, and emergent zero-shot medical reasoning. To further probe the capabilities and limitations of Med-PaLM M, we conduct a radiologist evaluation of model-generated (and human) chest X-ray reports and observe encouraging performance across model scales. In a side-by-side ranking on 246 retrospective chest X-rays, clinicians express a pairwise preference for Med-PaLM M reports over those produced by radiologists in up to 40.50% of cases, suggesting potential clinical utility. While considerable work is needed to validate these models in real-world use cases, our results represent a milestone towards the development of generalist biomedical AI systems.

keyInsights: A single generalist AI model can achieve competitive performance across diverse medical modalities and tasks, demonstrating the feasibility of unifying previously specialized systems.
Larger models exhibit emergent capabilities such as zero-shot generalization to new medical concepts (e.g., tuberculosis detection) and multimodal chain-of-thought reasoning.

Human evaluation by radiologists indicates that Med-PaLM M's generated reports are clinically useful, comparable to human reports in error and omission rates, and preferred in a significant portion of cases.
originalProblem: Traditional AI in medicine consists of isolated, specialized systems designed for single tasks or modalities, failing to reflect the integrated and multimodal nature of clinical practice.
Physicians routinely combine information from diverse sources (imaging, text, genomics), but existing AI solutions lack this comprehensive capability.
A lack of comprehensive benchmarks hindered the development and evaluation of generalist multimodal medical AI systems.
results: Med-PaLM M achieved state-of-the-art or competitive performance across all 14 diverse biomedical tasks within MultiMedBench using a single set of model weights.
Quantitative improvements included >8% improvement in micro-F1 for chest X-ray report generation and >10% improvement on Slake-VQA BLEU-1 and F1 metrics.
Human evaluation showed Med-PaLM M reports were preferred by radiologists in up to 40.5% of cases and had average clinically significant error rates (0.25 per report) comparable to human baselines.
solution: Google Research and Google DeepMind developed Med-PaLM M, a large-scale generative AI model built upon PaLM-E, capable of processing medical text, images, and genomic sequences.
The team created MultiMedBench, a comprehensive benchmark aggregating 12 open-source biomedical datasets for 14 distinct tasks, unifying them as open-ended text generation problems.
Med-PaLM M was trained end-to-end on MultiMedBench using instruction-based and one-shot learning to handle diverse tasks and generate textual responses.
paper_summary.summary: Google Research and Google DeepMind introduced Med-PaLM M, a generalist biomedical AI system capable of handling diverse medical data types and tasks with a single set of model weights. The system achieved competitive performance across 14 distinct biomedical tasks spanning medical imaging, clinical text, and genomics, and demonstrated emergent capabilities like zero-shot generalization and multimodal chain-of-thought reasoning validated through human expert evaluation.

title: Towards Generalist Biomedical AI

abstract: Recent advancements in prompt-based medical image segmentation have enabled clinicians to identify tumors using simple input like bounding boxes or text prompts. However, existing methods face challenges when doctors need to interact through natural language or when position reasoning is required - understanding spatial relationships between anatomical structures and pathologies. We present PRS-Med, a framework that integrates vision-language models with segmentation capabilities to generate both accurate segmentation masks and corresponding spatial reasoning outputs. Additionally, we introduce the MMRS dataset (Multimodal Medical in Positional Reasoning Segmentation), which provides diverse, spatially-grounded question-answer pairs to address the lack of position reasoning data in medical imaging. PRS-Med demonstrates superior performance across six imaging modalities (CT, MRI, X-ray, ultrasound, endoscopy, RGB), significantly outperforming state-of-the-art methods in both segmentation accuracy and position reasoning. Our approach enables intuitive doctor-system interaction through natural language, facilitating more efficient diagnoses. Our dataset pipeline, model, and codebase will be released to foster further research in spatially-aware multimodal reasoning for medical applications.

organization_info.name: Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications

keyInsights: Joint training of segmentation and position reasoning tasks significantly enhances the model's overall position reasoning capabilities, indicating a powerful synergistic effect.
Utilizing a lightweight vision encoder like Tiny-SAM provides a strong balance between performance and computational efficiency, crucial for medical applications.
Domain-adapted MLLM backbones like LLaVA-Med (with LoRA) outperform general-purpose MLLMs for medical tasks, validating the benefit of medical domain adaptation.
originalProblem: Existing prompt-based medical segmentation models lack semantic understanding of positional cues in natural language and struggle with implicit or conversational queries.
Current medical Vision-Language Models (MLLMs) can reason about images but often fail to generate precise segmentation masks, lacking spatial grounding for their textual outputs.
There is a critical scarcity of medical datasets with spatially-grounded question-answer pairs, hindering the development of advanced multimodal medical AI.
results: PRS-Med achieved state-of-the-art segmentation performance across six medical imaging modalities, including mDice of 0.817 for Breast Ultrasound, 0.803 for Brain MRI, and 0.968 for Lung CT-Scan, often significantly outperforming baselines.

The framework demonstrated superior position reasoning accuracy of 0.533 (averaged over Qwen and Llama 3.1 benchmarks), surpassing previous medical MLLMs like LLaVA-Med (0.419) and HuatuoGPT-Vision (0.437).

Ablation studies confirmed that the chosen Tiny-SAM vision encoder offers strong performance with significantly fewer parameters (31.49M) compared to Med-SAM (292.60M), and that the LLaVA-Med backbone with LoRA provides optimal performance for both segmentation and reasoning tasks.
solution: PRS-Med employs a three-component architecture: a lightweight TinySAM-based vision backbone, a LoRA-adapted LLaVA-Med for multimodal reasoning, and a prompt mask decoder to fuse features and predict segmentation masks.

A novel two-stage MMRS dataset generation pipeline leverages an LLM to create diverse, spatially-grounded Q&A templates by integrating positional information (quadrant, center proximity) extracted from existing segmentation masks.
The model is trained end-to-end with a combined loss function that optimizes both segmentation accuracy and textual position reasoning, fostering a synergistic learning process.
paper_summary.summary: A novel framework, PRS-Med, integrates vision-language models with segmentation capabilities to perform position reasoning segmentation in medical imaging, demonstrating superior performance across six diverse modalities while generating spatially grounded textual descriptions. It also introduces a scalable pipeline for creating spatially-aware medical question-answer datasets.

title: PRS-Med: Position Reasoning Segmentation with Vision-Language Model in Medical Imaging
